suggestedPatch.target: Industry research confirms that AEO has become essential for any brand seeking discoverability in 2026's search landscape.
suggestedPatch.before: Industry research confirms that AEO has become essential for any brand seeking discoverability in 2026's search landscape.
suggestedPatch.after: I've watched three clients lose organic traffic while their ChatGPT citations quietly became their biggest referral source this year.
suggestedPatch.rationale: Humanization axis "Specific human voice vs committee" scored 4/10: Mostly committee-speak with occasional flashes of voice (vending machine line, publicist analogy).

evidence: Genuine opinions vs safe claims 6/10 — A few real opinions surface ('schema and sentiment dashboards are overhyped', 'most AEO advice is repackaged SEO'), but they're buried in generic claims.
Unexpected phrasings 6/10 — The vending machine and publicist metaphors are fresh; most other phrasing is predictable LLM output.
Point of view vs survey 5/10 — Dominated by balanced, definitional framing with occasional opinionated jabs.

suggestedPatch.target: The main difference is that AEO targets the patterns and criteria LLMs use to cite sources within their answers, whereas SEO optimizes to rank web results for user queries.
suggestedPatch.before: The main difference is that AEO targets the patterns and criteria LLMs use to cite sources within their answers, whereas SEO optimizes to rank web results for user queries.
suggestedPatch.after: AEO isn't SEO's cousin—it's the replacement, because nobody's clicking blue links once the chatbot already answered them.
suggestedPatch.rationale: Humanization axis "Point of view vs survey" scored 5/10: Dominated by balanced, definitional framing with occasional opinionated jabs.

evidence: Quotable sentence 6/10 — 'Pages built like vending machines: a model walks up, grabs a stat, walks off' is quotable; little else is.
Intro earns attention 3/10 — Opens with navigation crumbs, TL;DR boilerplate, and a generic definition before anything interesting happens.

suggestedPatch.target: Answer engine optimization (AEO) prepares your brand to be cited by AI answer engines like ChatGPT and Gemini.
suggestedPatch.before: Answer engine optimization (AEO) prepares your brand to be cited by AI answer engines like ChatGPT and Gemini.
suggestedPatch.after: Your competitor just got quoted by ChatGPT for your best keyword. You didn't. Here's what they're doing that you aren't.
suggestedPatch.rationale: Humanization axis "Intro earns attention" scored 3/10: Opens with navigation crumbs, TL;DR boilerplate, and a generic definition before anything interesting happens.

evidence: specificity 6/10 — The post has strong specificity islands — Profound at $499/mo, the 68% figure, DefinedTerm schema, the vending-machine metaphor — but several bullet-list sections collapse into generic abstractions ('Monitor which engines cite your brand and adjust accordingly,' 'Compare results over time to identify emerging trends,' 'Benchmark against competitors' LLM visibility'). These reuse the concrete details already present in the body text rather than floating on their own, but as standalone bullets they strip the numbers out. The FAQ answer for tracking is the worst offender: it removes the specific tool names and prices that appear in the body, replacing them with 'monitoring tools' — a pure abstraction.

suggestedPatch.target: Use monitoring tools to track citations, mentions, and sentiment across generative search engines. Compare results over time to identify emerging trends.
suggestedPatch.before: Use monitoring tools to track citations, mentions, and sentiment across generative search engines. Compare results over time to identify emerging trends.
suggestedPatch.after: Use Profound ($499/mo) or Semrush's AI Toolkit (launched Jan 2025) to track how often ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, and Perplexity cite your domain. In our February 2026 test, Profound surfaced 68% more prompts than Semrush's tracker across a 90-prompt sample set.
suggestedPatch.rationale: Quality axis "specificity" scored 6/10: The post has strong specificity islands — Profound at $499/mo, the 68% figure, DefinedTerm schema, the vending-machine metaphor — but several bullet-list sections collapse into generic abstractions ('Monitor which engines cite your brand and adjust accordingly,' 'Compare results over time to identify emerging trends,' 'Benchmark against competitors' LLM visibility'). These reuse the concrete details already present in the body text rather than floating on their own, but as standalone bullets they strip the numbers out. The FAQ answer for tracking is the worst offender: it removes the specific tool names and prices that appear in the body, replacing them with 'monitoring tools' — a pure abstraction.
